scene_title: Another part of the heath. Storm still

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Blow, winds, and crack your cheeks! rage! blow!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You cataracts and hurricanoes, spout

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Till you have drench’d our steeples, drown’d the cocks!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You sulphurous and thought-executing fires,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Vaunt-couriers to oak-cleaving thunderbolts,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Singe my white head! And thou, all-shaking thunder,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Smite flat the thick rotundity o’ the world!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Crack nature’s moulds, an germens spill at once,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That make ingrateful man!

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: O nuncle, court holy-water in a dry house is better than this

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: rain-water out o’ door. Good nuncle, in, and ask thy daughters’ blessing:

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: here’s a night pities neither wise man nor fool.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Rumble thy bellyful! Spit, fire! spout, rain!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Nor rain, wind, thunder, fire, are my daughters:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I tax not you, you elements, with unkindness;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I never gave you kingdom, call’d you children,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: You owe me no subscription: then let fall

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Your horrible pleasure: here I stand, your slave,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: A poor, infirm, weak, and despised old man:

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: But yet I call you servile ministers,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That have with two pernicious daughters join’d

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Your high engender’d battles ’gainst a head

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: So old and white as this. O! O! ’tis foul!

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: He that has a house to put’s head in has a good head-piece.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No, I will be the pattern of all patience;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I will say nothing.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Who’s there?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Marry, here’s grace and a cod-piece; that’s a wise man and a fool.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Alas, sir, are you here? things that love night

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Love not such nights as these; the wrathful skies

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Gallow the very wanderers of the dark,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: And make them keep their caves: since I was man,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Such sheets of fire, such bursts of horrid thunder,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Such groans of roaring wind and rain, I never

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Remember to have heard: man’s nature cannot carry

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: The affliction nor the fear.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Let the great gods,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That keep this dreadful pother o’er our heads,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Find out their enemies now. Tremble, thou wretch,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That hast within thee undivulged crimes,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Unwhipp’d of justice: hide thee, thou bloody hand;

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Thou perjured, and thou simular man of virtue

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That art incestuous: caitiff, to pieces shake,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That under covert and convenient seeming

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Hast practised on man’s life: close pent-up guilts,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Rive your concealing continents, and cry

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: These dreadful summoners grace. I am a man

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: More sinn’d against than sinning.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Alack, bare-headed!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Gracious my lord, hard by here is a hovel;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Some friendship will it lend you ’gainst the tempest:

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Repose you there; while I to this hard house—

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: More harder than the stones whereof ’tis raised;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Which even but now, demanding after you,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Denied me to come in—return, and force

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Their scanted courtesy.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: My wits begin to turn.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Come on, my boy: how dost, my boy? art cold?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I am cold myself. Where is this straw, my fellow?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: The art of our necessities is strange,

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That can make vile things precious. Come, your hovel.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Poor fool and knave, I have one part in my heart

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: That’s sorry yet for thee.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: He that has and a little tiny wit—

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: With hey, ho, the wind and the rain—

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Must make content with his fortunes fit,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: For the rain it raineth every day.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: True, my good boy. Come, bring us to this hovel.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: This is a brave night to cool a courtezan.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: I’ll speak a prophecy ere I go:

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: When priests are more in word than matter;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: When brewers mar their malt with water;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: When nobles are their tailors’ tutors;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: No heretics burn’d, but wenches’ suitors;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: When every case in law is right;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: No squire in debt, nor no poor knight;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: When slanders do not live in tongues;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Nor cutpurses come not to throngs;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: When usurers tell their gold i’ the field;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: And bawds and whores do churches build;

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Then shall the realm of Albion

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Come to great confusion:

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Then comes the time, who lives to see’t,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: That going shall be used with feet.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: This prophecy Merlin shall make; for I live before his time.

stage_directions.text: The cod-piece that will house Before the head has any, The head and he shall louse; So beggars marry many. The man that makes his toe What he his heart should make Shall of a corn cry woe, And turn his sleep to wake. For there was never yet fair woman but she made mouths in a glass.

glosses.phrase: Blow, winds, and crack your cheeks
glosses.definition: Lear's command to the wind personified — the most famous line of the storm scenes
waterfalls; here, torrents of rain

flooded the church-towers

lightning-strikes that split oak trees
burn off my grey hair — Lear offers himself to be struck
flatten; strike level
the bulging roundness of the earth — flat the globe itself
break the templates of creation
seeds of life — spill (scatter, destroy) the very seeds from which creatures grow
ungrateful
flattery at court — better than the rain, the Fool quips
I don't blame you, storm-elements
submission; allegiance
slavish agents — the storm is working for his daughters
destructive; lethal
armies bred in the heavens — thunderclouds as soldiery
helmet — also the head itself
the decorative genital pouch on Renaissance breeches — the Fool's song makes it stand in for the penis / male sexuality
Lear addresses the guilty of the world in apostrophe
hidden sins
unpunished
murderer
counterfeit; pretender
unchaste in a close kinship sense; in Jacobean usage also broadly sexually transgressive beyond modern incest meaning
the bodies that hide you
process-servers of divine judgment
a wretched shelter — Kent offers Lear the only refuge on the heath
the Fool's mock-prophecy that follows — a parody of Chaucer's 'When priests are more in word than matter'

glosses.phrase: priests are more in word than matter
glosses.definition: the Fool's Chaucer-parody opening — the world turned upside-down as moral diagnosis
wizard of Arthurian legend — the Fool places his prophecy in a pre-Merlin past
Lear's derisive license to the storm — growl all you want
I don't accuse the elements — they are innocent
vassal allegiance — the storm owes him nothing

armies bred in the heavens
indeed — oath
the Fool's proverb: the truly prudent do not enter weather like this without a hat

unscourged by law

courage

the craft of making do with what we have
the Fool's mock-prophecy: when rich men count coins publicly

annotations.title: The storm as externalised psychology
annotations.body: Lear's 'Blow, winds, and crack your cheeks! rage! blow!' is the moment critics have most often used to argue that Lear is not merely tragic drama but poetic cosmology. The storm is the play's most famous example of pathetic fallacy turned structural: Lear's mind is the weather. Harold Bloom writes that these scenes contain 'the greatest poetry ever composed' and that Lear 'is not so much a character as a cosmic force.' The storm is also staging itself against the theatrical limits of Shakespeare's stage — a king on a literal heath in literal wind and rain, calling on the elements to destroy the templates of creation ('Crack nature's moulds, an germens spill at once'). This is metaphysical revolt dressed as meteorology. Lear wants the world un-made, and the play permits him the syntax for it.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: 'I am a man more sinn'd against than sinning' — compressed moral accounting
annotations.body: After the big apostrophe to the storm, Lear quietens into a different register: 'Let the great gods, / That keep this dreadful pudder o'er our heads, / Find out their enemies now. Tremble, thou wretch, / That hast within thee undivulged crimes / Unwhipp'd of justice.' The list — hidden criminals, perjurers, adulterers — is almost a litany of Jacobean moral anxiety. Lear's own confession, 'I am a man / More sinn'd against than sinning,' is the play's quietest moment of self-appraisal. Not 'innocent,' not 'wronged' — he admits sin while claiming the weight is uneven. That asymmetry is the play's tragic judgment: Lear is no saint, but what's been done to him exceeds any reasonable punishment. This is a surprisingly rare move for Shakespeare — a protagonist who concedes fault while refusing the full allocation of blame.

annotations.title: The Fool's prophecy — a Chaucer parody amid the storm
annotations.body: Left alone on the heath, the Fool delivers a mock-prophecy beginning 'When priests are more in word than matter.' The speech is a parody of a genuine medieval prophecy commonly attributed to Chaucer, inverting its logic — the Fool prophesies a world in which justice reigns, which is by definition impossible. The joke is serious: the Fool is saying that Britain will never see such a world, and a kingdom that must wait for universal virtue for reform will wait forever. Some editors (Folger, Riverside) note that the prophecy may have been inserted by an actor later and question Shakespeare's authorship. Either way, it ends with 'This prophecy Merlin shall make; for I live before his time,' a temporal joke that makes the Fool older than Arthur and places his pessimism outside history.
sources: G. Blakemore Evans, The Riverside Shakespeare (1974, 1997)

trials.prompt: Critics often describe the storm in 3.2 as —
options: Externalised psychology — Lear's mind made weather; pathetic fallacy raised to structural principle

trials.prompt: Harold Bloom's reading of the storm scenes treats them as —
options: 'The greatest poetry ever composed,' with Lear 'not so much a character as a cosmic force' — the summit of Shakespearean verse

trials.prompt: Lear's 'I am a man / More sinn'd against than sinning' concedes what?
options: That he has sinned (not claiming innocence), but that what has been done to him exceeds the weight of his own faults — an asymmetric moral judgment
That he is guilty of everything

trials.prompt: The Fool's prophecy at the end of 3.2 parodies —
options: A genuine medieval prophecy commonly attributed to Chaucer, inverted for pessimistic effect
